name: ESXi Coredump Partition Not Configured
description: An ESXi host has a persistent health warning because no coredump target is configured, leaving the host unable to capture crash data if it hits a PSOD.
customerScenario: One of our ESXi hosts has had a yellow warning in the host summary for weeks. It says something about a coredump partition not being configured. We've been ignoring it but our manager noticed and wants it fixed before the next change window. We don't know what a coredump is or whether it matters.
customerFollowUp: We've never had a host crash, so we've always assumed these warnings were cosmetic. But apparently our last VMware support case got escalated because we couldn't provide a crash dump when requested. I think that's related to this.
fixSteps: In vSphere Client, go to the affected host > Monitor > Health and confirm the coredump configuration warning
SSH to the host and run: esxcli system coredump partition list
If a local diagnostic partition is listed but not active, activate it: esxcli system coredump partition set --partition <partition-id> --enable true
If no local partition is available, configure a network coredump target: esxcli system coredump network set --interface-name vmk0 --server-ip <ip> --server-port 6500
Enable network coredump: esxcli system coredump network set --enable true
Verify: esxcli system coredump partition list — the active partition should show as enabled
Confirm the health warning clears in vSphere Client (may take a few minutes)
hints: A coredump warning means the host has no configured destination to write a crash dump if it hits a PSOD (Purple Screen of Death). VMware Support requires crash dumps to diagnose host failures. Which command-line interface gives you full control over ESXi system configuration, including diagnostic settings?

SSH to the ESXi host and use esxcli system coredump to inspect and configure the coredump target. Start with: esxcli system coredump partition list — this shows available diagnostic partitions and whether any are currently active.

ESXi supports two types of coredump targets: a local diagnostic partition on a disk attached to the host, or a network coredump collector. If a partition appears in the list but is not active, you can activate it without any additional hardware.

If a local partition is listed but inactive, activate it with: esxcli system coredump partition set --partition <id> --enable true. The partition ID comes from the output of the list command. If there is no local partition, you need a network coredump collector.

Activate the local partition: esxcli system coredump partition set --partition <partition-id> --enable true. Verify with esxcli system coredump partition list that it now shows as enabled. The health warning in vSphere Client should clear within a few minutes.
learningObjectives: Identify a missing coredump configuration via the ESXi host health check warning
Use esxcli system coredump commands to list and activate diagnostic partition targets
Distinguish between local partition and network coredump collector options
Understand why coredump configuration is a prerequisite for VMware support escalation
author: vSphere Lab Wizard starter library
